name: Spor Mountain
short_description: BLM public land site in Juab County known for fluorite, beryl, and topaz crystals. Free rockhounding with no permit required - perfect for collectors seeking colorful mineral specimens.
description:
Spor Mountain is a popular rockhounding destination on Utah BLM land in Juab County, offering collectors the opportunity to find vibrant fluorite, beryl, and topaz specimens. The site sits within the Basin and Range province where Tertiary-age volcanic activity and hydrothermal mineralization created ideal conditions for fluorite and beryl deposition in fractures and vugs throughout the mountain.

The collecting area features exposed rock formations where mineral specimens can be found scattered across the surface and in shallow diggings. Fluorite specimens often display striking purple, blue, and green colors, while beryl crystals appear as greenish hexagonal forms. Topaz crystals and quartz specimens are also present, making this a diverse site for both casual collectors and serious hobbyists.

As a BLM public land site, Spor Mountain requires no permit and has no entrance fee, making it accessible to rockhounds of all experience levels. The elevation and semi-arid terrain mean weather conditions vary significantly by season. Spring and fall offer the most comfortable collecting conditions, though winter snows can occasionally block access.

This location is best suited for rockhounds willing to do modest surface collection and light digging. The terrain is rugged but navigable, and the variety of minerals makes every trip rewarding. Pack water, sunscreen, and appropriate tools - the remote location means services are some distance away.
history: Spor Mountain sits within the Basin and Range province of central Utah where Oligocene-age volcanic and hydrothermal activity created widespread mineralization. Fluorite, beryl, and topaz formed in fractures and cavities within rhyolitic tuff and other volcanic host rocks as hot, mineral-rich fluids cooled during the waning stages of igneous activity approximately 30-35 million years ago.
tips: Search the exposed hillsides and eroded areas where fluorite and beryl crystals weather out of the surrounding rock matrix - look for purple/blue fluorite cubes and greenish beryl hexagonal crystals. Use a small pry bar or flathead screwdriver to carefully extract specimens from cavities without damaging the delicate crystal faces. Focus your efforts on areas showing visible mineral-filled veins or vugs in the light-colored volcanic rock, and always check layered zones where mineralization tends to concentrate.
terrain_notes: Spor Mountain is located at approximately 6,500 feet elevation with rolling, semi-arid terrain covered in sparse sagebrush and scattered juniper. The collecting area involves hiking and scrambling over uneven rocky slopes with moderate elevation gain. Most productive areas are within 0.5 to 1.5 miles of parking. Footing is rocky and irregular with loose talus sections requiring careful navigation. No shade available, so plan accordingly.
best_season: April - May, September - October. Spring and fall offer pleasant temperatures and low precipitation. Summer heat at this elevation can reach 85-90 degrees F with intense sun. Winter snow may block access or make terrain difficult from November through February.
written_directions: From Nephi (nearest substantial town, approximately 35 miles south), take US Route 6 northeast toward Helper for approximately 25 miles. Turn north toward the Tintic Mining District area and follow local county roads toward Juab County BLM lands. The Spor Mountain collecting area is accessed via unmarked BLM roads - current topographic maps and GPS coordinates are essential. Approach via 4WD or high-clearance vehicle is recommended for winter and spring conditions when roads may be rough.
road_conditions: Rough dirt and rocky BLM roads, potentially impassable after rain or snow. High-clearance or 4WD vehicle recommended.
parking_notes: Informal parking areas near the collecting site on BLM land. No developed parking lot or facilities. Space is adequate for several vehicles.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 pounds per day per Federal Land Policy and Management Act (FLPMA). No commercial collection. No motorized excavation equipment permitted. Use hand tools only. All collection must be for personal hobby use.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 pounds per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection or sales
No motorized excavation equipment
Hand tools only
No collecting in restricted or posted areas
Leave the site as you found it
nearest_services: Nephi, Utah (approximately 35 miles south) offers gas, food, lodging, and water. Mona, Utah is closer (approximately 15 miles south) with limited services. Stock up in Nephi before heading to the site.
accessibility_notes: Uneven, rocky terrain with moderate elevation gain. No wheelchair access. Sturdy hiking boots required. Terrain involves scrambling over loose talus and navigating around rock outcrops. Not suitable for those with limited mobility or altitude sensitivity.

faq.question: What minerals can I find at Spor Mountain in Utah?
faq.answer: Spor Mountain is known for purple, blue, and green fluorite crystals, greenish beryl specimens, topaz crystals, and clear quartz. Fluorite is the most common find, appearing as well-formed cubic crystals on the hillsides. Collectors regularly recover specimen-quality pieces suitable for display.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at Spor Mountain?
faq.answer: No permit is required. Spor Mountain is on BLM public land in Utah with free access for personal rockhounding use. You can collect year-round as long as you follow the 25-pound daily limit per FLPMA regulations and do not use motorized equipment.

faq.question: What is the best time to visit Spor Mountain for rockhounding?
faq.answer: April through May and September through October offer the most comfortable collecting conditions. Daytime temperatures are mild (60-75 degrees F), and precipitation is minimal. Summer heat can exceed 85 degrees F with intense sun exposure. Winter snow may block access from November through February.

faq.question: Is Spor Mountain rockhounding good for beginners?
faq.answer: Yes, Spor Mountain is beginner-friendly. Minerals weather out of the rock naturally and can be found on the surface or with modest digging using hand tools. No special skills are required, and the variety of specimens keeps the experience rewarding for collectors of all levels.

faq.question: What tools should I bring rockhounding at Spor Mountain near Nephi?
faq.answer: Bring a small pry bar, flathead screwdriver, or old knife blade for extracting crystals from cavities. Hammer and chisel are useful for careful removal. A small brush helps clear dust from specimens. Always use hand tools only - motorized equipment is not permitted on BLM land.

faq.question: How far is Spor Mountain from Nephi, and is a high-clearance vehicle necessary?
faq.answer: Spor Mountain is approximately 35 miles north-northeast of Nephi, Utah. Access via rough BLM roads makes a high-clearance or 4WD vehicle highly recommended, especially during spring snowmelt or after rain when roads become difficult. Passenger cars may struggle with the terrain.

meta_description: Find fluorite, beryl, and topaz at Spor Mountain on Utah BLM land. Free rockhounding, no permit required. Public land near Nephi.
